I've looked into ruamel.yaml and I definitely appreciate the fact that upstream PyYAML is on life support/nearly dead. Map ordering is preserved by both yq and jq. As you correctly point out, preservation of comments is irrelevant since JSON does not admit comments and jq does not support non-standard JSON dialects that do. The main reason to use ruamel.yaml is actually its support for YAML 1.2, which PyYAML does not support and which eliminates some annoying unused misfeatures like "sexagesimals" that interfere with reasonable YAML use cases.

With that said, I am not yet comfortable switching to ruamel.yaml because I don't consider that project to be sufficiently mature. The ideal situation would be if the key features introduced by ruamel.yaml were ported to and released in PyYAML by its new maintainers.

PyQt has this convenience feature that when you import `PyQt5.Qt` it will import all classes under the same namespace. So even if you aren't using `QtQml`, it would still get imported, which I am just assuming adds to the amount of time it takes to import.

I'm sure there is reference of it in the documentation, but couldn't find it just now. This was the closest thing I found.

- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/21323899/import-pyqt4-does-not-include-pyqt4-qtcore-or-qtgui

To solve this, I would rather find a way of only importing what the user actually imports.

```python
from Qt import QtWidgets
# Should not import QtCore, QtQuick.. etc.
```

Alternatively if we test and make sure it actually does take longer, maybe it doesn't, in which case there's nothing to worry about.

Hi, thank you for posting the question. For all rulestes (including flowchart), the state is checkpointed (stored in redis) after an action is executed. In addition an event is emitted so you can write rules to reason about the state. In the case of flowchart, a stage is an action execution.

If the same ruleset is run in multiple hosts, using the same redis instance, actions will be executed in any host (the first available). The framework guarantees a single action execution at any point in time for a given sid (state id). An action will be considered abandoned after 15 seconds.

OK, so after some fiddling I worked this out -- sorry, I'm no oauth expert, but maybe some others looking at this thread aren't, either.

The token returned in the cookie from:

https://accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/programmatic_auth?client_id=936475272427.apps.googleusercontent.com&scope=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.google.com%2Faccounts%2FOAuthLogin+https%3A%2F%2Fwww.googleapis.com%2Fauth%2Fuserinfo.email

is an auth token, not a refresh token. You can't just paste it into refresh_token.txt.

What I did was was short circuit _get_authorization_code in auth.py and set the authorization_code directly to the value returned in the programmatic_auth page. The code then goes through the rest of _auth_with_code and writes a new refresh token that works.

> trying to get the .text of a stream doesn't seem the best idea :D

I suppose. :) Note that the same error happens to `.content`, which just passes through the StreamBody when there's no `content-encoding`. 

> requests allows `.text`ing here

I see two ways to handle this:

1. Enforce the streaming context and deny access to the 'unstreaming' properties, gracefully, early, and consistently. This forces the client code to be mindful of the situation and protects from errors down the road.

2. Transparently 'unstream' and decode, to keep the interface consistent.

I agree that (2) seems unnecessary, given that streaming is always a concious choice by the user. Anyway, in either situation it might help to have a different response class for streaming. It keeps things neatly separated code-wise, so nobody needs to check if there's a StreamBody present. (At least, a `response.is_streaming` might come in handy.) The stream response could just not have the questionable properties in the first place.

I don't know the reasons why `requests` chose (2); they might be good reasons. :) Anyway, `asks` doesn't need to mirror requests in every detail. But the choice should be made, to make the interface consistent and its behavior easier to predict.

I believe I'm running into the same problem now as @goretkin.  Here's my very simple setup:
I run tshark on the commandline with very restrictive bpf and display filters.  These filters cause tshark to emit only TCP packets with content when talking to a specific HTTP service.  I run tshark manually with these filters in one terminal.  In another terminal, I run my own pyshark-based script that uses the same filters as well as the "-l" option.

When I hit the HTTP service in my browser, tshark shows 6 packets:  2 HTTP request packets, and 4 HTTP response packets.  There are two HTTP requests because my browser is sending my desired request as well as a request to /favicon.ico.  (Each individual HTTP response consists of two packets because the HTTP response headers are sent separately from the body.)  The two requests are sent at virtually the same exact time.  The two response packets are also sent at the same exact time for a given response (same tcp timestamp).

However, in my pyshark-based script, I see only 3 packets: one request and two response packets.  This is alarming.

Looking into it further, I ran both sniffers continuously and hit refresh in my browser twice.  the first time, my pyshark script printed out only my desired HTTP request (as just described). However, upon hitting refresh again, pyshark printed out the /favicon.ico request and associated response.  So this tells me the data is clearly being captured, but is getting stuck in a queue somewhere and only fetched when my second browser refresh happens.  Repeated refreshing causes the printed packets to alternate between the two request types.  This overall behavior seems consistent with the described bug.  HTH.

I don't know the overall program at all but I see the issue: the first branch has:
`if len(raw_colors) > 16`
and the second does:
`if len(raw_colors) < 16`
But we need `len(raw_colors) < 17`, because further down we do:
`return [re.search("#.{6}", str(col)).group(0) for col in raw_colors[1:]]`
If `raw_colors` had exactly 16 elements, it'll end up spitting out a palette of 15 elements at the end and that's where the index error is coming from.

Depending on what the `re.search` is doing, either:
1. Change it to iterate over `raw_colors` and not leave out the first element, or
2. Change the error checking condition to require a minimum of 17 elements.

Well, lets go one step at a time, otherwise I just stare at a huge long list, and go ..' errr .... some time, maybe' :-P

So, 'a.) A simple example of populating a set of Multi-class training feature vectors in DeepCL's internal format'.  A few places you can get this from:
- https://github.com/hughperkins/DeepCL/blob/master/src/main/train.cpp#L177-L194
- this calls GenericLoaderv2, which handles a variety of formats
- actually, this is just a wrapper around various per-format loaders.  You can see the wrapping bit here https://github.com/hughperkins/DeepCL/blob/master/src/loaders/GenericLoaderv2.cpp#L33-L39
- GenericLoaderV1Wrapper is just a loader around the earlier GenericLoader https://github.com/hughperkins/DeepCL/blob/master/src/loaders/GenericLoaderv1Wrapper.cpp#L41
- GenericLoader (ie the v1 loader) handles a bunch of formats https://github.com/hughperkins/DeepCL/blob/master/src/loaders/GenericLoader.cpp#L37-L48
- ManifestLoader is for reading huge directories of images, ie for imagenet, with a single manifest file describing the whole tree of images https://github.com/hughperkins/DeepCL/blob/master/src/loaders/ManifestLoaderv1.cpp

So, pick one of these loaders, and look how it works.  Alternatively, basically there are two sets of data:
- input data features.  These are in a 1-dimensional vector, laid out as `[n][plane][height][width]`
- labels.  These are also a 1-dimensional vector, of labels (0-based, I think)

I have a few solutions, however I'm not sure which is best.

The first and easiest (although most horrible) is to patch https://github.com/jmcarp/flask-apispec/blob/master/flask_apispec/wrapper.py#L43 to set format_response to the 'identity' function, which simply returns the value passed to it with no changes made.

Or, the 'APISPEC_FORMAT_RESPONSE' config option can be set before the flask app is started, to a function that takes one argument and returns the value of that argument (flask_apispec.wrapper.identity or a lambda).

Another solution is to patch both the flask.jsonify so that it accepts a new kwarg, status_code:
```
def jsonify(*args, status_code=None, **kwargs):
```

... and marshal_result() in flask_apispec.wrapper so that it returns:
```
format_response(output, status_code=status_code)
```
Returning a single Response object (and not a tuple) means that flask-restful will handle it correctly and the response successfully makes it onto the wire.

Anyway,

Q1. The best way to do this is to build up the list in a loop and then use that in `write_rich_string()` outside the loop.

Q2. Text wrapping can be applied for normal or rich strings using a format with `text_wrap` see the [docs]( http://xlsxwriter.readthedocs.org/format.html#set_text_wrap).

It is possible in `write_rich_string()` to add a format to the cell, rather then the string, by putting it at the end. See the [write_rich_string](http://xlsxwriter.readthedocs.org/worksheet.html#worksheet-write-rich-string) docs.

For example in the [following example in the docs](http://xlsxwriter.readthedocs.org/example_rich_strings.html) the rich string is centered using a center format.

Okay, so what I did to **use Textract to extract PDF using pdftotext on Windows with Pyinstaller**:

- Set up `pdftotext.exe` somwhere so that it's executable for Python.
  - Option A: Add it to the pyinstaller `.spec` file like this `datas=[('pdftotext.exe', '.')],`
  - Option B: Add it to the Windows PATH environment
- Configure PyInstaller
  - Add the parsers you want to use to the `.spec` file in the `hiddenimports` section e.g. `hiddenimports=["textract.parsers.pdf_parser"],`
  - *( Not sure if required )* Add `pathex=['PATH\\TO\\Python\\Python36\\Lib\\site-packages\\textract\\parsers'],`

*I only have very basic experience using pyinstaller, this might be a wrong/bad way to do it, but it works.*

I ran into this issue while trying to do a simple `pip install watchdog` in a virtualenv with Python 3.6.5 on my mac.

Turns out in `setup.py` [line 34](https://github.com/gorakhargosh/watchdog/blob/master/setup.py#L34) where the platform is detected as OSX it's adding this compile arg `-Wno-error=unused-command-line-argument-hard-error-in-future` which is not supported by the gcc shipped as default with older Xcode installs.

Since I am using homebrew gcc (`gcc version 8.1.0 (Homebrew GCC 8.1.0)`) instead of Xcode, to fix this I had to skip that check on L34 by changing the string `macosx` to something else like `macosy` and then run `python3 setup.py install` which resulted in successful installation.

The problem is that parglare doesn't really know anything about associativity and operators. :)

Keyword `left` is just a synonym of `reduce` (see note in [this section](http://www.igordejanovic.net/parglare/disambiguation/#associativity)) which means "if you are at this production and token ahead if of the same priority use reduce instead of shift" which is essentially a behaviour that leads to left associativity for operators of the same priority.
Priorities are honoured first.

Since ADDOP and MULOP are separate rules with two productions each and their priority is not explicitly set it is given a default priority of 10 which is higher than priority set for STMT productions.
This means that the token ahead will always have a greater priority and lead to shift, .e.g right associativity.

Yes. If you are use operator terminal inline as in the last example, than everything will work as expected as the terminals will be given the same priority of the STMT production.

Second example is something that will be preferred way to do it when #17 is done.

Several users of [tetherback](https://github.com/dlenski/tetherback) have been running into this issue as well: [tetherback #20](https://github.com/dlenski/tetherback/issues/20), [#21](https://github.com/dlenski/tetherback/issues/21).

I can generate it reliably under Ubuntu 15.10 amd64, python 3.5, by running the following script which generates random `max_value` from a log-normal distribution, and then updates the progress bar at random intervals:

```python3
from progressbar import ProgressBar, Percentage, FileTransferSpeed, ETA, DataSize
from time import sleep
from random import lognormvariate, normalvariate
from math import log

for ii in range(100):
    max_value = lognormvariate(log(1000000), log(500000))
    print('{}: max_value={}'.format(ii, max_value))
    
    pbwidgets = ['  test %d: ' % ii, Percentage(), ' ', FileTransferSpeed(), ' ', ETA(), ' ', DataSize() ]
    pbar = ProgressBar(max_value=max_value, widgets=pbwidgets).start()

    cur_value = 0
    while cur_value < max_value:
        pbar.update(cur_value)
        sleep(normalvariate(.2, .05))
        cur_value += max_value/100 * lognormvariate(0, 1)
    pbar.finish()
```

```python
1. / (1 + (a / (b + 1)) * ((alpha + T) / (alpha + recency)) ** (r + 1))
```

has a lower bound of `1. / (1 + (a / (b + 1))`. In your case, it is about 0.30.

There are two separate cases: whether the package being checked ("top") is the argument given to `caniusepython3` (`dd` in case 1 [above](https://github.com/brettcannon/caniusepython3/issues/155#issue-173385516)) or not:

- if it is, and we add Python 3 to the top package, then none of the dependencies that we wanted to check is ever checked. So, `caniusepython3` would answer "yes" for any package whose `setup.py` we run it on, as long as the package lists Python 3. But the purpose of running `caniusepython3` on a `setup.py` is to start by checking the dependencies, not by starting with the top package's Trove classifiers. So, if truncation happens somewhere, then that should be below the top package, from its descendants and lower.

- if not, then the usefulness I see is in verification of the job that others have done. If listing Python 3 in a package's Trove classifiers on PyPI was possible only if all its immediate dependencies also listed Python 3 in their Trove classifiers, then that would be an inductive invariant on the dependency graph (assumed acyclic) of packages on PyPI.
  
  In that case, yes, I would agree that there is little point in changing the truncation. However, that is not how things work (it could be something for `warehouse` to *require* when uploading a package, but the current implementation of PyPI does not enforce this -- as far as I know). Therefore, I cannot be sure that others (or myself) did remember to check what their dependencies declare.

  So, the benefit is to check properties of the dependency graph that nobody may have bothered to check yet, something that a developer cannot be sure, unless they maintain their entire dependency graph. The overrides file is an explicit way to record deviations from the standard, when those are detected.

  At the very least, a developer who detects such a lack of classifiers can contact the maintainers of the offending package, and note the absent classifiers. In this way, some projects may be reminded to update their classifiers, or perhaps even to re-examine the assumptions they made about their dependencies.

I think that the first case is not what one expects to check with `caniusepython3`. The second case is more on the completeness side, with the benefits noted. If performance is a concern, then a complete check could be made optional, via a command line argument.

This is what worked for me on Ubuntu 16.04 to connect to MS SQL Server:

System dependencies:
sudo apt-get install unixodbc unixodbc-dev freetds-dev freetds-bin tdsodbc

Python dependencies
pip install pyodbc

Configuration
Add this to /etc/freetds/freetds.conf:

```
    [<server_name>]
            host = <host name or IP address>
            port = 1433
            tds version = 7.0
```
  
Test with:

` $ tsql -S <server_name> -U <username> -P <password>`
            
            
Add this to /etc/odbcinst.ini
    
```
    [FreeTDS]
    Description = TDS driver (Sybase/MS SQL)
    # Some installations may differ in the paths
    #Driver = /usr/lib/odbc/libtdsodbc.so
    #Setup = /usr/lib/odbc/libtdsS.so
    Driver = /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/odbc/libtdsodbc.so
    Setup = /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/odbc/libtdsS.so
    CPTimeout =
    CPReuse =
    FileUsage = 1    
```      


Add this to /etc/odbc.ini
```
    [sqlserver]
    Driver = FreeTDS
    Description = ODBC connection via FreeTDS
    Trace = No
    Server = <host name or IP address>
    Port = 1433
    Database = <db name> 

```
    
Test with:

`    $ isql -v sqlserver <DBuser> <DBpass>`


Try connecting with pyodbc:

```
import pyodbc

dsn = 'sqlserver'
user = ' ... '
password =  ' ...'
database = ' ... '

con_string = 'DSN=%s;UID=%s;PWD=%s;DATABASE=%s;' % (dsn, user, password, database)
cnxn = pyodbc.connect(con_string)
cnxn
```

Try connecting with sql extension:

%sql mssql+pyodbc://<username>:<password>@sqlserver

                obj_type=obj_type,
                content=r.content,
                code=r.status_code)

Sorry for the missing information at the docs and not being able to write before.

Due the way python and gevent works, the pool is shared between threads (or greenlets) inside the same python process, a connection can't be shared with other process. Remember that with gevent, one process can use hundred or thousand of connections at the same time, this pool allows to set a limit per process and also avoid django DB queries to block the process that will make gevent useless. If you want a global pool, pgbouncer or similar should be used.

So, MAX_CONNS should be set to (postgres's connections allowed / number of processes) or lower.

If I understand correctly, you want the behaviour of the Create/UpdateWithInlinesViews, but without the parent form?

The closest to this at the moment would be InlineFormSetView, which gives you a single formset related to a parent object. I've just realised that this view doesn't put the parent object into the context. This is an oversight, its desired behaviour to behave like all other things inheriting from SingleObjectMixin and to put object and optionally the result of get_context_object_name() into the context.

In the mean time, you can start with InlineFormSetView and override get_context_data to add self.object into the context.

Thanks to this issue, I've realised that I've missed out the use case of having multiple formsets in this manner (ie without displaying the parent form).

I can approach this in one of two ways:

1. Modify InlineFormSetView to use an inlines field, much like CreateWithInlinesView. This would avoid the creation of a new view, but would break backwards compatibility
2. Add a new view, called DetailWithInlinesView (as you suggested) or similar.

In the use case you emailed me about, where you only need to show one inline, using InlineFormSetView and overriding get_context_data should do the trick. Until I fix the bug, at which point you won't need the override anymore.

> Following Django's form validation for standard full unique constraints, it would check if another row with the same group exists in the database, and raise error if yes.
> For the partial unique constraint validation, we have to raise the error only if also is_complete = 0 on the new object. Because you can insert a duplicate group if the new object doesn't match the where condition, even if some existing ones do.
> But parsing the referenced fields from the text-based where conditions is tricky.

Yeah, actually attempting to parse the text would definitely be the wrong approach. I was thinking more along the lines of using a `Q` object to set the constraint. This should be compilable to a SQL expression, both as part of the constraint or when performing the check. For complex situations, one might still drop down to using a `RawSQL` object instead of a `Q`.

`catkin_lint` has a special rule to ignore executable files in the `cfg` subfolder, which AFAIK is the recommended file system location for `dynamic_reconfigure` configuration files.

`catkin_lint` complained for the wrong reasons, but the issue is that your package uses a file that is not part of the package and depends on a very particular filesystem layout. If you were to release your package as official ROS package, you could no longer rely on `global_config.cmake` being where you expect it.

Still, you can override `catkin_lint`'s complaint. Starting with the next release, add the line `#catkin_lint: ignore external_file` before that `include()`. For now, you can silence the error with `#catkin_lint: ignore missing_file`.

@windcheck as a point of clarification, `pipenv run` is not really a pipenv python shell -- to get teh same behavior you need to spawn an actual shell using `pipenv shell`.  This is because, when executing `pipenv shell` we toggle `PYTHONUNBUFFERED` if necessary to ensure that you don't get buffered standard output writes, whereas `pipenv run` always keeps python buffered due to the fact that it isn't running an actual subshell. If you need a full pty, you need to use `pipenv shell`

The generic update (cli_generic_update_command) is built to handle most of the “normal” (and recommended pattern) from ARM – i.e. the model type returned by a GET is the same as the model type for the body parameter for PUT. This is used in cases where PATCH is not supported (most services). If this is *not* the case, you are officially in custom command land for your update command. 

In addition, there are some cases where the parameter flattening kicks in and the PUT ends up with all method parameters where the generic update command will not work as well. But, again, there is nothing stopping you from implementing a custom command in these cases.

@williexu So it seems doc is not clear. According to my understanding 5000 is the limit regardless of --num-results being used or not. Doc states that if request does not specify --num-results or specifies a value greater than 5000, server will return 5000.

--num-results
Specifies the maximum number of blobs to return, including all BlobPrefix elements. _If the request does not specify num_results or specifies a value greater than 5,000, the server will return up to 5,000 items_. Setting num_results to a value less than or equal to zero results in error response code 400 (Bad Request).

That's why the paging capability that the API exposes is missing in the CLI. In such scenario, results would be delivered in batches of up to 5000 per iteration.

This fails when `conda.resolve.Resolve.bad_installed` is called at https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.5.3/conda/core/solve.py#L219. `context.track_features` is first considered only later on at https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.5.3/conda/core/solve.py#L301.
Since `bad_installed` sees that the installed `testpkg` has `features=gcc540` but there is no accompanying `track_features=gcc540` package installed and `context.track_features` isn't yet factored in, `testpkg=1=gcc540_0` is considered as "inconsistent":
```
DEBUG conda.resolve:bad_installed(759): Limiting solver to the following packages: 
DEBUG conda.resolve:bad_installed(761): Packages to be preserved: [Dist(channel='file:///path/to/conda-test-chan', dist_name='testpkg-1-gcc540_0', name='testpkg', version='1', build_string='gcc540_0', build_number=0, base_url=None, platform=None)]
DEBUG conda.core.solve:solve_final_state(236): inconsistent dists: 
  - file:///path/to/conda-test-chan::testpkg-1-gcc540_0
```
Thus, it is "preserved", i.e., excluded from the subsequent steps.

@argltuc: Until this is fixed, you could create a package with `track_features` and install that as a substitute for the configuration parameter.

@wulmer thanks for all the great feedback.  The present mess with backslashes is my confusion over when they are escape characters, and when they are literals.  When passing anything to shell subprocesses, I think we need 4 (first set of escapes are Python, next set of escapes are shell).  When we print stuff, there should be only 2 (resulting in just one being displayed).

```
from glob import glob

for path in glob('/Users/aatish/anaconda/conda-meta/*.json'):

        with open(path) as fh:
            data = loads(fh.read())
    except:
        print(path)
        raise

It is a "special property" of tree models (and their ensembles such as Random Forest models) that the size of the model object increases as the size/complexity of the dataset increases.

To solve the problem, you must change [the parameterization of `RandomForestClassifier` object] (http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.ensemble.RandomForestClassifier.html) so that the learning algorithm would limit the complexity of tree objects:
* `max_depth`. Assuming binary split, there could be `2^max_depth` nodes in the final tree.
* `min_samples_split`
* `min_samples_leaf `
* `max_leaf_nodes`.

For example:
```
clf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators = 100, max_depth = 10, min_samples_split = 100, min_samples_leaf = 5)
```

I would advise you to increase the number of estimators (ie. set the `n_estimators` parameter to much greater value than 10), and focus on configuring the size of individual estimator trees.

It's not PMML problem _per se_. For example, if you simply serialized your RF models in Python's native pickle data format, then you'd see exactly the same happening - a small dataset would give you a small pickle file, whereas a large dataset would give you a much bigger pickle file.

Thanks @Ichimonji10 for the feedback. I think your concerns are very real and something we want to solve before we go down this path of allowing pulp-smash tests live inside plugins. Some thoughts below.

One question I have is: does Pulp QE want to maintain smash tests for all plugins? With Pulp 3 I think we'll see more community-contributed plugins like [pulp_gem](https://github.com/ATIX-AG/pulp_gem). If plugins start writing smash tests with our current model, PulpQE is going to need to review these PRs, maintain their code, etc. Seems like a better model would be to put the onus on plugins to manage their own smash tests.

> Pulp Smash and pulp_ansible are frequently present on different hosts.

What if in this case we install two copies of pulp_ansible—one on each host?

> The Pulp 3 installer (both from-source and from-PyPI) install Pulp and its plugins into non-standard paths.

Yea, we would need to deal with this.

> Pulp Smash would need to be re-designed to support namespace packages.

Doesn't Pulp Smash already namespace plugin tests? Also, I think this responsibility would fall on plugins if they want to write pulp-smash tests so not sure if it's something QE needs to worry about? 

> Pulp Smash has no stable API.

What about a semantically versioned API? This might be a good compromise between stability and the freedom for pulp-smash to innovate/improve.

> Splitting code across repositories means that the linters and unit tests built in to Pulp Smash won't be able to work their magic on the separated code.

In our PRs, we install pulp-smash into Travis and we could certainly run any linters or unit tests on any code changes to the pulp-smash tests inside pulp_ansible or whatever plugin. The reverse (adding new linters, etc) in pulp-smash could be a pain though.

> Pulp Smash is a non-trivial application. It's reasonable to think that its requirements and pulp_ansible's requirements might conflict, meaning that installing them into the same virtualenv won't work.

Yes, agreed. This is something we'll have to solve although I think running pulp-smash tests against each PR would hopefully reveal such problems as they arise.

ConjunctiveGraph requires the default graph to be the merge of all graphs, which is not necessary for Dataset. So, I would recommend to use the SPARQL store only with Dataset. Also, the developer should be aware that the behaviour of the default graph of such a Dataset may be different for different endpoints.

Hey,
hmmm....getting the largest possible value that the forest can predict is a tricky number to compute. For a single tree, this is trivial to compute, but for the forest things are more complicated. It's quite likely that the highest prediction of individual trees do not occur for the same input (due to the randomization during training). It is not ever guaranteed that the highest prediction is for any of these inputs. Maybe I misunderstand what you are trying to do, so could you please elaborate on what you mean by "max possible value" that the forest can predict?

Regarding you questions:
- No you can't get all the internal nodes with the Python API. The C++ internals are usually not of interest, so I never exposed any of that.
- The string representation is only for when you want to pickle the forest. The names `valuex` come from the serialization library I use, and carry no meaning. You can reconstruct the object from the string though, but it's hopeless to try to find all the leafs from that representation. 
- You could get all the nodes and then get the `response_stat` to compute the largest output for a tree, but as I argued above, that is not necessarily the true global maximum.

Hope that helps.

    docker-machine create -d virtualbox experimental
    docker-machine ssh experimental 'sudo sh -c "set -ex; /etc/init.d/docker stop || true; sleep 2; curl https://experimental.docker.com/builds/Linux/x86_64/docker-1.9.0-dev > /usr/local/bin/docker; /etc/init.d/docker start"'
    eval "$(docker-machine env experimental)"

Then update `DEFAULT_DOCKER_API_VERSION` to `'1.21'` in `docker/constants.py`.

I have a update on this for the current version of .NET Core (Version 2.0.3).

On Windows, if you install this version of the .NET Framework then the normal Microsoft symbol server http://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols can find all the necessary symbolic (PDB) and annotated with SourceLink information to do source stepping in both the .NET Runtime Framework (CoreFX) as well as ASP.NET by fetching the symbolic information from that server and the source code from the appropriate GIT repository.  

The upshot of this is that if you debug a .NET Core application using version 2.0.3 of the runtime (e,g. from https://www.microsoft.com/net/download/windows) and do the following 

1. Uncheck the (Tools -> Options -> Debugging -> Just My Code) checkbox.
2. Insure that the (Tools -> Options -> Debugging -> Symbol Settings -> Microsoft Symbol Servers) checkbox is set
3. Insure that the (Tools -> Options -> Debugging -> Enable Source Link support) checkbox is checked

Then you should be able to step into (or set breakpoints in) Framework or ASP.NET code and the source code will automatically be brought up.   

Note that the framework code is precompiled and optimized by default, which means that stepping and local variable access in framework code may not be nice as you would like (variables may be unavailable)

For anyone who encounters this issue and would like some more specifics.  In `build_act` you need to change the `_act` function to evaluate the `q_values` as well

The last few lines of `build_act` should be 

```python 
_act = U.function(inputs=[observations_ph, stochastic_ph, update_eps_ph],
				 outputs=(output_actions, q_values),
				 givens={update_eps_ph: -1.0, stochastic_ph: True},
				 updates=[update_eps_expr])
def act(ob, stochastic=True, update_eps=-1):
	return _act(ob, stochastic, update_eps)
return act
```
so that you get the values and not the tensor. 

Then anywhere where you call `act` you will have to go one index deeper.  For example in `simple.py` line 252 `action = act(np.array(obs)[None], update_eps=update_eps, **kwargs)[0]` should be `action = act(np.array(obs)[None], update_eps=update_eps, **kwargs)[0][0]`

So it turns out you can do this yourself (without any pybind changes) with just two lines:

```C++
PYBIND11_MAKE_OPAQUE(std::shared_ptr<void>);
```
and then in your definitions somewhere:
```C++
py::class_<std::shared_ptr<void>>(m, "encapsulated_data");
```

That's actually a bit easier than doing it in pybind11 itself: we can't encapsulate the pointer in a plain python capsule because we also have to preserve the `shared_ptr<void>` instance (because its destruction might invalidate the pointer).  So it really has to be a wrapped type, i.e. we have to wrap the `std::shared_ptr<void>`, not just the `void *`, and so we need a custom type.

> Sure, but looping is still something that people will want to do reasonably fast. Checking attributes at every iteration is a good way to kill performance.

But computing byte offset from scratch on every iteration is not optimal regardless. If you have a tight loop, the chances are you'll fetch a pointer once anyway and then increment the strides manually in the loops to avoid needless multiplications and stride fetching? NumPy C API has some macros (`PyArray_ITER_*`) to simplify iteration, maybe instead of removing bound checks we could provide iteration primitives?

The documentation about this macro already reads (and has, since 1.1):

> To enable transparent conversions for functions that take shared pointers as an argument or that return them, a macro invocation similar to the following must be declared at the top level before any binding code:
>
> PYBIND11_DECLARE_HOLDER_TYPE(T, std::shared_ptr<T>);

Think of the new message as being a compile-time check that you actually declared the holder type before attempting to use it.

the default function lets you extend what is serializable by defining the JSON version of any object.  For example:

```python
from datetime import datetime

ISO8601="%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S.%f"

def date_default(obj):
    """default function for packing timestamps in JSON."""
    if isinstance(obj, datetime):
        return obj.strftime(ISO8601)
    else:
        raise TypeError("%r is not JSON serializable" % obj)

from zmq.utils import jsonapi

jsonapi.dumps(dict(now=datetime.now()), default=date_default)
```

It's not a problem for send_json itself, which doesn't expose kwargs, but honestly, send_json shouldn't be relevant for anyone who actually cares about control or performance - it's really just there for convenience for simple applications, because the two serialization libraries in the stdlib are pickle and json.  

The only reason I added support for *any* extra json libs is that the version of simplejson in the stdlib in Python 2.6 and (to a lesser extent) 2.7 is so amazingly slow that I just couldn't stand it.  And the reason that more than one is supported is there didn't seem to be a convincing single answer for reasonably fast alternatives, because even though simplejson had improved, it remained quite slow.

Honestly, the most important value of jsonapi is ensuring the right bytes/unicode behavior on Python 2 and Python 3, not performance, and a consistent interface for that is very important (and the reason I should have dropped jsonlib1 long ago).

Thanks for the warning about cjson, I am aware of its state, and have no intention of ever supporting it.

It is so that the registry can bring it's exceptions with it so in user code you can do

```
try:
    db.reg.register_handler(...)
except db.reg.DuplicateHandler:
    ...
```

and now you don't have to import `databroker` in your user code (if you assume one will be passed into you).   This is a pattern that came from before the big split, but gives us a layer of indirection if we (or one of our collaborators) want to implement a completely independent version of `Databroker` that just duck-types correctly.

Here are the CPU speed tests on a c5.xlarge. Although unlikely as a production deployment, I also added `batch=10` numbers out of curiosity. These are the setups (couldn't get the colours to match the previous graphs, sorry!):

* The red and orange lines correspond to baselines in master, with the commit ID in brackets.
* Purple and blue are the batched topK implementations that @fhieber referenced above.
* The green line is a two-step topK explained below.

## Split by batch size

![cpu_speedbybatch](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6504048/38799628-491b78ba-4165-11e8-854d-5ae5bf9cd019.png)

## Split by beam size

![cpu_speedbybeam](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6504048/38799640-50440ddc-4165-11e8-8498-2fe8e6bba331.png)

## Discussion

* The mxnet batched topK turns out worse in all cases except `beam=1,batch=1`.
* The numpy batched topK seems slightly better in the `batch=1` case (as CPU translation would probably be deployed), especially in `beam=5`.

Given that the greedy setting (`beam=1`) is unlikely, I would go for the numpy-based batched topK for CPU inference and keep the mxnet-based one for GPU. What do you think? I can update this PR with the code after.

On Ubuntu 16.04, `systemctl` uses `apachectl` to manage the apache daemon. It directly calls (more or less) `apachectl start` and `apachectl stop` to start/stop the apache daemon.
apachectl manages the apache daemon by invoking the apache2 binary located at `/usr/sbin/apache2`.
So, it doesn't really matter which is used to start/stop apache at least on Ubuntu.

Unfortunately, the strings used to denote these various bounds are not really indicative of what they represent. I'll see if I can think of better strings to use. What's confusing when doing branch and price is that there are multiple bounds one has to consider, as opposed to the single bound one obtains by solving the LP relaxation in branch and cut. 

Here, `LB` and `UB` are the upper and lower bounds on the optimal value of the full relaxation associated with this particular node (what we would have if we knew all the columns). These bounds are obtained by solving the current restricted master.  Each time we solve the restricted master problem, the optimal solution value we obtain is an upper bound on the optimal value to the full relaxation. To get a _lower bound_ on the optimal value of the full relaxation, we have subtract the reduced cost of the column with most negative reduced cost with respect to the current master. We only obtain a good estimate of the actual lower bound yielded by the node `LB` and `UB` are close together, i.e., the most negative reduced cost column has a reduced cost near zero. If the reduced cost of the most negative reduced cost column is exactly zero, we have an exact value for the bound yielded by solving the relaxation in this node, but in practice, we won't get to exactly zero due to numerical inaccuracies and so-called `tailing off` effects.

To complicate matters, the sequence of `LB` values is not necessarily monotonic, so the current `LB` value may not actually be the best lower bound on the relaxation that we have seen so far. `nodeLB` is the best lower bound seen so far while processing this particular branch-and-bound node. `gLB` is the current global lower bound (the minimum node lower bound over all nodes). `gUB` is the global upper bound, which is the objective value of the best solution found so far. The algorithm as a whole terminates when `gLB` (approximately) equals `gUB`.

Phase has to do with two things: whether the algorithm is currently generating cuts or columns and whether it's in phase 1 (trying to obtain an initial feasible solution) or phase 2 (trying to improve the objective value). `PHASE_PRICE2` means that it's phase 2 and it's generating columns. `p` and `c represent the number of rounds of column generation (pricing) and cut generation (cutting) so far.

It's difficult to say exactly what's happening in this particular case without doing a bit more digging, but a few things are evident. First, `LB` and `UB` are not very close to each other, which means that the most negative reduced cost column generated in the last round had a reduced cost that wasn't anywhere close to zero. Thus, the column generation is still producing columns that appear to be important. On the other hand, the current lower bound is far from the best one seen so far, which means that you are probably experiencing some pretty severe fluctuations in the lower bound produced by the master in different iterations. This is a pretty well-known phenomena with column generation and is the motivation for the development of various stabilization techniques (_dual smoothing_ and _dual stabilization_ in the literature). It's caused be oscillations in the dual solutions being produced when solving the restricted master. Because we are using the simplex algorithm, we always get extremal solutions, which may exacerbate the problem. 

Since there have been 383 rounds of column generation and convergence doesn't seem to be anywhere in sight, it looks like you are in fact experiencing some pretty severe oscillations in the dual solution value. You could try just turning on DIP's dual smoothing, which is based on a simple technique of Wentges. The relevant parameter is `DualStab` and you can try just setting it to `True`. I haven't played around much with this, but it might work.

If this doesn't work, then there are other possibilities, but nothing has been implemented yet. If you are willing to share your instances, we can try to take a look and see what's happening.

The way you rebase usually is the following:

```
$ git rebase -i origin/master
```
Editor open with the list of commits
```
pick 1fb2288 Edit see also box in doc
pick 86b9b0b set dirty flag on kernelspec/language_info changes
pick c9a9b5b don't overwrite file
pick ac509bc fix login page UI problem
```

You delete the lines of the commit you don't want, (follow the instructions)
save and quit editor.

```
$ git rebase -i origin/master
Rebasing  (9/15) # updates while rebasing
Successfully rebased and updated <your branch>
```

If there are conflicts it will tell you "conflict in ....", you edit the file(s) to resolve conflict, git add the files, and then `git rebase --continue` a conflict look like that

```
<<<<<<<< fix these -> this
THere is 2 conflicts in this sentence
=======
There is 2 conflits in these sentence
>>>>>>>> Lowercase H
````

As a human you know how to merge the two, and delete the `<<<<<`,`>>>>>`,`======`. Anyway rebase might look scary, it is not, you can alway get back on your feet.

I had the exact same error on my Mac. Why did it request `en-gb` locale?
It turned out the code uses `navigator.language` property and in my chrome console it indeed showed `en-GB`. In my case I expected `en-US`.
So, if you are in US locale, not in UK, and on a Mac, the solution is: go to System Preferences -> Language & Region, there replace `English` with `English (U.S.)` in Preferred languages.
This solved the issue for me.

```bash
echo "${GPG_PASSPHRASE}" | gpg2 --batch --import key.asc

touch dummy.txt
gpg --batch --yes --passphrase-file key.txt --pinentry-mode=loopback -s dummy.txt # sign dummy file to unlock agent
# Decrypt secret
sops -d values-dev-enc.yaml > values-dev.yaml
```

I'd like to reopen this issue.
Here is my use case:
I need to run ~5000 jobs in batfish using 12 parallel Z3 worker threads at a time.
I have 64GiB of RAM on my machine, and have set the -Xmx60g jvm flag.
The output of each job is stored in memory and not garbage collected, but the output is relatively small. I believe the saved output from each job is <1KiB.
When I run the whole thing at once as described, batfish crashes with an out-of-memory exception after **several hours** with full CPU utilization on all 12 cores.

To work around this, I partitioned the jobs into 13 more or less equally-sized chunks of jobs, to be run separately. Each chunk was run in a separate process, also with 12 parallel Z3 worker threads.. The chunks were run one after the other. All chunks were completed after only **20 minutes**.

I assume that the crashing case took much longer for 2 reasons:
(1) Instead of just instantly freeing the memory associated with a chunk of several hundred jobs when the process ended, the memory had to be meticulously freed by the garbage collector.
(2) Old contexts leaving uncollectable garbage eventually reduced the maximum available free memory for new jobs, forcing more and more frequent garbage collection.

We can potentially address (1) by reducing the complexity of garbage collection, e.g. by using PhantomReferences instead of finalizers as alluded to in #442 by cheshire.
I believe that such a solution may also alleviate (2) if engineered properly. However, I do not know enough about the difference between finalizers and PhantomReferences to say so authoritatively.

The current example and @asvetlov's are very clear in showing how you can chunk files.

The problem is that usually in other frameworks you don't loop or iterate on an object to get your params and the same is also true for aiohttp, with `Request.query` and `data = await request.post()`.

My problem was that you jump from this example

```python
    mp3 = data['mp3']

    # .filename contains the name of the file in string format.
    filename = mp3.filename

    # .file contains the actual file data that needs to be stored somewhere.
    mp3_file = data['mp3'].file

    content = mp3_file.read()
```

to this example

```python
async def store_mp3_handler(request):

    reader = await request.multipart()

    # /!\ Don't forget to validate your inputs /!\

    mp3 = await reader.next()

    filename = mp3.filename

    # You cannot rely on Content-Length if transfer is chunked.
    size = 0
    with open(os.path.join('/spool/yarrr-media/mp3/', filename), 'wb') as f:
        while True:
            chunk = await mp3.read_chunk()  # 8192 bytes by default.
            if not chunk:
                break
            size += len(chunk)
            f.write(chunk)

    return web.Response(text='{} sized of {} successfully stored'
                             ''.format(filename, size))
```
and you completely miss what's going on with `next` because there isn't a context anymore.
The short paragraph before the example talk about the multipart reader and if you follow the [link](http://aiohttp.readthedocs.io/en/stable/multipart.html#aiohttp-multipart) everything is discussed apart from `Reading Multipart Request`.

After I checked the implementation of `Request.post` everything was clear but I agree is too verbose to just paste the code.

I think that a simple comment will make the example more clear.
Something like this:

```python
    async def store_mp3_handler(request):

        reader = await request.multipart()

        # /!\ Don't forget to validate your inputs /!\

        # reader.next() will `yield` the fields of your form

        field = await reader.next()
        assert field.name == 'name'
        name = await field.read(decode=True)

        field = await reader.next()
        assert field.name == 'mp3'
        filename = field.filename
        # You cannot rely on Content-Length if transfer is chunked.
        size = 0
        with open(os.path.join('/spool/yarrr-media/mp3/', filename), 'wb') as f:
            while True:
                chunk = await field.read_chunk()  # 8192 bytes by default.
                if not chunk:
                    break
                size += len(chunk)
                f.write(chunk)

        return web.Response(text='{} sized of {} successfully stored'
                                 ''.format(filename, size))
```

So, pf route-to should bypass the routing table and route the packet through the interface specified in the rule. FreeBSD and OpenBSD behave as expected but in OSX the routing table is used and the route-to rule is ignored.

Opened bug 27061884 with Apple.

Can be reproduced without sshuttle or openvpn with:
```
# Add a route for a subnet <A> through some interface (other than lo0)
sudo route add -net <A> -interface en0
# add the following pf rules, where <B> is a subnet in <A>
sudo pfctl -e
sudo pfctl -f /dev/stdin
table <forward_subnets> {<B>,!127.0.0.1/8}
rdr pass on lo0 proto tcp to <forward_subnets> -> 127.0.0.1 port 12300
pass out route-to lo0 inet proto tcp to <forward_subnets> keep state

nc -l 12300
curl -I <B> -vvv
# nothing gets routed to nc
```

Hey @iorbitearth!

Great to hear that you're interested. I believe I've had similar conversations with @henryhallam from Planet, and that there are a lot of overlapping use cases for what's a pretty general problem for embedded binary protocols.

Regarding the generator itself: a lot of the existing code is very hacked together. We're planning on having an actually engineered solution (i.e., a flexible compiler), but probably not anytime soon. At least from the academic point of view, I'm definitely interested in hearing more about what you'd like to do with what's there and probably wouldn't mind collaborating on improvements.

That being said, certain parts of the framing protocol are actually flexible. The python and C libraries both have framing stuff separated out (see https://github.com/swift-nav/libsbp/blob/master/python/sbp/client/framer.py), but there are obviously lots of assumptions about what fields are present, etc. You could likely implement your own framer and your own `SBP`-like parent object that your message types could inherit from. Once you get to that point though, SBP (and PlanetBP) becomes instances of some more-general binary protocol compiler that should live on its own.

By the way, there's some related prior art with similar ideas, but are also worth reading about:
- https://github.com/janestreet/bin_prot
- https://people.csail.mit.edu/nickolai/papers/bangert-nail-langsec.pdf
- https://github.com/jbangert/nail
- https://github.com/abiggerhammer/hammer

Nail is probably the most similar, but seems to have a less usable message grammar than what's currently in `spec/`.

@mfine, @JoshuaGross : thoughts?

Indeed: ``factory.Faker.override_default_locale()`` is a decorator and context manager: you can only use it as:

```python
with factory.Faker.override_default_locale('es_ES'):
    e1 = ExampleFactory()
```

Or:

```
@factory.Faker.override_default_locale('es_ES')
def test_foo(self):
    e1 = ExampleFactory()
```

The idea is that changing the default locale is mostly used for a few specific tests while the whole suite doesn't care; hence, having a decorator/context manager ensures that the default locale is restored once those tests have run.

However, this is not very clear in the docs; I think we should improve them.
Alternatively, we could add an extra entrypoint to set the global, initial configuration for Faker (including adding extra providers).

I subscribe to this. IMHO, the ApplicationRunner does to much of magic (well, it's not magic at all, the code is very short and easy to understand, but you have to check what is going on here when you are discovering autobahn-python), i.e. running the loop and closing the loop. I think this should be done explicitly by the user (or there should be a way to do it manually, if the default is doing all the machinery of the loop) because, when writing software that has already asyncio components, it's a bit strange not being able to start the loop by yourself, or adding an autobahn component to an already running loop/software, more over, when the connection to the router is lost, the complete program stops (because the loop is stopped), and, if i.e. you want to do asyncio things to clean stuffs (i.e, start an asyncio commit to db, continue running tasks and restart a new session,...) you can't.
I know you can subclass ApplicationRunner to change all those things, by I'm more or less convinced that's what will happen to the majority of users, rewriting ApplicationRunner in a way or another, so, maybe there is some value to rethink the thing.
I also find the documentation should emphase ApplicationRunner is designed for quick tests and development and is likely to be subclassed for more complex setup.

Hi all, I've been giving this some thought, apologies but I've made a few drafts of this message already and it gets difficult to sort out.

##### Problem

The problem is that ```isCompatible``` function is a tad aggressive with backwards compatibility imo.
We don't want to cause a job not to be backwards compatible due to some extra config or metadata being lost imo, but we also don't want to lose this information if we can help it.


If a schema is loaded and the object on disk has extra information this information is being destroyed by the fact that this is not stored within the schema.

However, I still think a sensible thing is to lock older/newer jobs on disk in 6.1.15 if we cannot load/modify them. I strongly think we should consider bumping some hard versions come Ganga 6.2 to try and enforce a forward only move if it's needed.
I tried doing this with 6.1 but there were technical reasons why I decided not to go down this route.

A more helpful error such as 'please use an older/newer (as appropriate) version of Ganga to modify this job' when we can't load a job would be nice.


##### After looking at the ```isCompatible``` function:

I think that we should change this to support minor version changes and define these as changes  which should NOT cause the job to become un-loadable by earlier versions of the Schema.
Also, we should continue to should not support major version changes which could potentially be a complete rewrite of the class and/or schema.
If a schema is changed in future such that loading the job will cause the XML structure to change then this should always be a major change imo. I can't think of a good reason why we would need more than major and minor version increments.


##### Proposed solution
Firstly I think we need to encourage developers to use these more and one barrier to this is we're lacking the ability to load 'newer' semi-compatible jobs with 'older' schema versions of Ganga imo.

It would be nice to store any (unknown)extra schema attributes in a read-only dictionary within the schema which is stored and re-written to disk along with any possible future changes to the job.
(and of course un-copyable so this is lost when the job is finally destroyed)
This is very possible but it requires some work to be done with the ```Schema``` and ```GangaObject::append``` functions within the Core.

*Unfortunately, this is completely incompatible with the ```is_prepared``` property for applications but this is a problem for another day and in another issue...*

##### Aside:

I know it's only an example but to be completely honest I'd really like 6.1.14/15 to be released so that people will stop talking about 6.0.44 as maintaining this as well now is just not feasible for much longer.

##### addendum

@egede Yes this is potentially a major change here as ideally old jobs would be modified in such a way that old versions of code couldn't read the XML...
I'd like to re-structure some of the XML handling code to potentially allow for some 'easy' migration tools to be written in the future.
I would like to be able to load older jobs with a dataset generator to bring the advantage to older jobs but doing this could take a lot of development time which may be easily mitigated by encouraging users to update and resubmit.

now that pantsd's transitive options dependencies are in bootstrap options and the daemon launches from a clean memory state, I think the core problem here should be as easy as fingerprinting a specific set of bootstrap options and comparing that to a resident fingerprint in the running daemon.

the fingerprint would include:
- options that affect pantsd (workdir, processdir, etc)
- options that affect pantsd services (pailgun host/port, fs event workers, etc)
- options that affect any transitive components of pantsd services (like the scheduler services scheduler or watchman)
- the pants version

the fingerprint would be computed and then checked in the pailgun service's runner_factory when the thin client is in synchronous contact with the daemon via the pailgun. in the event of a fingerprint miss, a notification would be sent to the thin client to instruct it to disconnect, restart pantsd and reconnect (likely using a custom extension to the nailgun protocol).

I /think/ we'll only have to worry about task and subsystem options as we begin to move their execution/initialization/storage into the v2 scheduler.

I think the first step is splitting out a separate tool/main from the zinc wrapper called something like `zinc-bootstrapper`.

In the short term, we could invoke the `zinc-bootstrapper` tool locally, always, and cache it the same place we currently do (in `HOME`). In the longer term we'd want to execute it as a separate remote process to handle the caching.

@PrinceKaro said: 

> also keep in mind that I never had any problems 
> running livestreamer which is very similar.

livestreamer on Windows uses Python 2.7, 
whereas streamlink is using an embedded 
edition of Python 3.5.2; this fact alone 
changes minimum system requirements 
and adds the dependency to which @beardypig 
directed you.

It might be that an overzealous A-V suite is 
blocking the streamlink NSIS installer. 
First re-install the MSVC++2015u3 runtime 
(file vc_redist.x86.exe) with your A-V temporarily 
disabled - this is a MS file, IT'S SAFE. Then reboot. 

Then try to re-install latest nightly version from [here](https://streamlink-builds.s3.amazonaws.com/nightly/windows/streamlink-latest.exe), 
again with your A-V suite temporarily disabled - 
I think you can trust the dev team in that 
they have not packaged anything neferious...

Post here any eventual errors during installation - 
then reboot again for your registry and PATH 
to be updated. 

One last thing that I've seen it happen on 
[another project](https://squarepenguin.co.uk/forums/thread-1113-post-4978.html) is that your PATH may already be overcrowded or corrupt; 
in a command prompt execute `echo %PATH%` 
and kindly post here for the devs to inspect; 
it may be that some leftovers need be removed...
Again, many overzealous antivirus apps, through 
their "system-monitoring" modules, have been known 
to deny other apps from modifying PATH; AFAIK, 
even MS's Windows Defender / MSE do the same 
and you have to OK manually the modification...

The Queue algorithm above requires that the put lot use strict FIFO, to avoid weirdness where we wake up the wrong put task. (Like, if you have a capacity 0 queue, it should never be the case that a task is still asleep after its particular value has been returned by `get`, or that a task can wake up before its particular value was returned by `get`.) Though I guess we could also skip the lot and have a deque of put tasks.

Oh ugh, it also makes `put` cancellation like... impossible to support.

I guess we could use a queue that maps tasks to the queue values, though this is problematic for the tasks that have woken up (and particular if they want to queue another item!). So I guess it would have to be

```python
self._queued = deque()
self._put_waiting = OrderedDict()
```

and `get` tries to pull from `_queued`, and if that works it refills from `_put_waiting`. Otherwise it tries to pull from `_put_waiting` directly, to support capacity=0, then if that doesn't work it sleeps.

Or... we can tweak it. `get` tries to move one item from `_put_waiting` to `_queued`, ignoring the capacity limit in `_queued` (because it will always try to remove an item from `_queued`, so even if it adds an item this can't cause its size to increase). Then it tries to remove an item from `_queued`. If that fails, it sleeps. When it wakes up, it decrements the `_get_waking` counter, and repeats the dequeue dance (which this time must succeed)?

While the underlying C library has pause/resume support this is not currently exposed in the Python bindings.

But since you don't need to call poll() to keep heartbeats alive in the Python client do you really need to pause?
Do note though that if you don't call poll() until your processing is done you will not only delay the next message but also any callbacks that might be triggered, such as rebalance (on_assign/revoke, stats, on_commit, ...etc).

I spoke with Magnus briefly about this and this does appear to be the case and its by design. 

My previous comment is a bit misleading so allow me to clarify along with the reasoning for this behavior in librdkafka. 

When I said "wait for a single message " what I really meant is a single message could reset the timeout if the previous fetch did not satisfy the `num_messages` limit. 

Since messages are fetched and enqueued, in batches each iteration of the loop could satisfy your batching requirements. In the event the number of records fetched could not it will loop back around and try again for another full timeout. In other words `timeout_ms` is actually more of an idle timeout then a batching timeout. If the loop can't make any progress within the configured threshold it will return leaving you with a partially filled buffer. 

The documentation could be more clear on this in both librdkafka and python. I'll make a note of it to get that clarified.

The practice of retagging existing releases was mostly useful for integration with ROS. Since at the moment parta of the ROS debian package is maintained inside librealsense master branch, whenever release is scheduled, the package gets updated shortly after without any changes to functionality. While minor bug fixes were sometimes addressed between retags, they were mostly included by mistake. I personally prefer bumping the minor version.
To eliminate this practice I'm working with @mdhorn on splitting ROS debian into a seperate branch with distinct releases.

I have the same problem on my computer (but not all the time, and mainly when used in multiple camera configuration).
Someone from my team solved the problem by creating a script to reset the usb bus before starting the program. If you want avoid to reset the whole bus, it's also possible to simulate a usb disconnect-reconnect by getting the bus and device id (lsusb -d 8086:0a80 for R200, 8086:0aa5 for SR300, 041e:409b when the camera is wrongly recognized as a Creative camera), and using this program to do the reconnection : https://gist.github.com/x2q/5124616
and using it like sudo ./usbreset /dev/bus/usb/002/007 (replace 002 by your bus id and 007 by your device id)

GraphiQL is a standalone application, it doesn't require any integration. I have an API built on top of Flask and using GraphiQL in the same application for exploration the API. Basically all I had to do was to adapt the [official example](https://github.com/graphql/graphiql/blob/master/example/index.html) - change the URL for `fetch` in `graphQLFetcher` function. If GraphiQL is to run on the same domain, it's probably all you have to do. If it runs on different domain, you'll have to enable CORS and that's it.

I've found a tricky way to get the new edge, which may help.
use ```offset_to_cursor```  in ```graphql_relay.connection.arrayconnection```.
Here's an example:
```python
class AddTodo(relay.ClientIDMutation):

    class Input:
        text = String(required=True)
        
    viewer = Field(User)
    todoEdge = Field(relay.types.Edge.for_node(Todo))
    
    @classmethod
    def mutate_and_get_payload(cls, input, info):
        viewer = get_viewer(info.request_context)
        todo = viewer.todos.create(text=input.get("text"))

       # get cursor here!
        cursor = offset_to_cursor(viewer.todos.count() - 1)

        edge = relay.types.Edge.for_node(Todo)(node=todo, cursor=cursor)
        return AddTodo(viewer=viewer, todoEdge=edge)
```

403s will only capture some of the bans, and will fail to work correctly in many cases. Replicating the crawlera ban detection logic, and/or exporting the rules from crawlera, opens them up publicly. This is not something we want to do, but is unavoidable if this approach is to work.

Another drawback is how does this get enabled? Presumably a user must experience a ban somewhere first, then think to search for a solution or contact support? We can't preemptively do it since it will incur a cost and may delay or break some existing code. If we want users to enable it, we need to ask them and it may be irrelevant to them, or add a barrier somewhere we don't want one (e.g. upon signup).

I'd like to propose that we do not add this functionality here.

How about instead we send the user a notification when a job experiences a ban? Perhaps via intercom? We could provide instructions on how to sign up to crawlera, if needed, or just a link to enable for that spider and reschedule if desired. The ban detection could be implemented using the requests stored in hubstorage and the crawlera rules. It won't work for the case where the body of the request is needed, but this is very rare and there are work arounds.

The advantage of this approach is that we're telling the user about Crawlera at the precise time that it is useful to them. We can reuse our Crawlera rules and not worry about them leaking externally.

There are three ways I have thought of to provide sort keys, and they all have drawbacks as far as BTrees itself goes. 

I'll prefix this by saying that I feel that providing a comparison function (`cmp`) is right out. That pattern is deprecated in Python 2 and gone in Python 3 (as @mdedmin reminded us), and it would be unPythonic to try to revive it publicly.

So, implementations. The first way is to copy `sorted` and `list.sort` and accept a `key` function for *everything* that might need to do some comparisons. That includes both `__setitem__` and `__getitem__` for the `tree[key]` and `tree[key] = value` cases. As BTree is a dict-like object, and a dict-like object only accepts one parameter for those two methods, that just can't work, unless you start down the path of "magic" key values that the BTree knows to inspect and split into a key and a function pair...and nobody wants to deal with that. Nobody wants to deal with having to pass a key function every time they try to subscript a tree either.

The second way would be to make the key function a property of the BTree itself. That avoids the dict-like issues, it's true. But lambda functions can't be pickled, so the key function would have to be named as a module global---and at that point you're right back to "it makes code refactoring always require database updates" (which isn't strictly true, there are ways to manage that pretty cleanly, starting with leaving BWC imports in place moving up to `zope.deprecation`). It's not clear to me that a module global function is any better than a module global class with custom comparison functions anyway. (This approach has the secondary problem of needing a new pickle format for the tree, which is complicated but possible, I think.)

The last way I thought of is to define a `_sort_key` instance method for OOBTree and have it internally be called every time the BTree needed to do a comparison (which is a lot). Then you could subclass the OOBTree and override that method. This has performance implications even for those trees that don't use it, which is not good (the C implementation uses a macro to inline the call to the C API comparison function, and this would add the substantial overhead of calling a python function,  and it's non-trivial to make sure it's called the absolute minimum number of times; the previous approach could probably do a null-pointer check to avoid this overhead but I don't think that's possible with a potential subclass method). Plus, that's right back to the "it makes code refactoring always require database updates" concern, and once again, it's not strictly clear to me how that's any kind of an improvement over just implementing the comparison in the datatype---surely if they're meant to be sortable in a BTree, it would be good to be able to sort them other places too?

The BTree approach (and indeed, the most general Python approach) to sorting non-orderable objects is to have the user define the order by implementing the appropriate comparison methods. That seems more sound to me than any of the options I discussed above.

If one can accept having custom objects live in the ZODB, then I think code compatibility can easily be kept by using a wrapper (similar to zope.container) around the OOBTree that checks incoming keys to see if they are the appropriate namedtuple comparable subclass, and if not, converting them.

By descriptive labels, do you mean ``col_id`` (aside: should probably be ``col_ids`` since this is an array)?

If so, then I agree it's important to list the col_ids in the feature labels. The issue is that there isn't really a good framework for this.
* Currently, most featurizers with this kind of complication assume that you will call featurize() before feature_labels(). The featurize() might store some information (like col_ids). Then the feature_labels can reference this information. This has issues, see #135 
* Your solution is for feature_labels to also take in the same type of arguments as featurize. This is possible, but for many featurizers (not yours) it would require essentially re-processing the data in order to figure out the feature labels. This is because many feature labels depend on both the rows and columns of the dataframe - different rows give different features. I'd like to avoid this as a long-term solution since it "fixes" your case but not others.

I think we should maybe move that discussion to #135

You can create your own field types by wrap existing field types and changing them to be required by default:
```python
def required_field(field_type):
    class RequiredField(field_type):
        __name__ = 'Required' + field_type.__name__
        def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
            kwargs.setdefault('required', True)
            super(RequiredField, RequiredField).__init__(*args, **kwargs)

    return RequiredField

import marshmallow as m
import marshmallow.fields as mf

String = required_type(mf.String)
Integer = required_type(mf.Integer)
# ...

# all fields required by default
class UserSchema(m.Schema):
    username = String()  
    email = String()
    age = Integer()
    full_name = String(required=False)
```

```python3
def _wait_for_deployment_complete(deployment_name, timeout=60):
    api = _get_kube_api(client.ExtensionsV1beta1Api)
    start = time.time()
    while time.time() - start < timeout:
        time.sleep(2)
        response = api.read_namespaced_deployment_status(deployment_name, K8S_API_NAMESPACE)
        s = response.status
        if (s.updated_replicas == response.spec.replicas and
                s.replicas == response.spec.replicas and
                s.available_replicas == response.spec.replicas and
                s.observed_generation >= response.metadata.generation):
            return True
        else:
            print(f'[updated_replicas:{s.updated_replicas},replicas:{s.replicas}'
                  ',available_replicas:{s.available_replicas},observed_generation:{s.observed_generation}] waiting...')

    raise RuntimeError(f'Waiting timeout for deployment {deployment_name}')
```

The problem you are seeing is related to zip (egg) files.  The dynamic import library wasn't designed with that in mind; however, its not a difficult addition to make.   Using __import__ is okay but it only imports the __init__.py file so if you have a package it could cause unintended behavior and is relatively frowned upon by PEP302.  Of course all of this goes away in py3 as the import libraries are *substantially* better.

Taken from __import__ docstring

```
Import a module. Because this function is meant for use by the Python interpreter and 
not for general use it is better to use importlib.import_module() to programmatically 
import a module.
```

I can update the loader to support zip files hopefully soon, but wanted to document here so every one knows what the problem is and if someone can tackle it sooner great.

ref: https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0302/
ref: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/28962344/imp-find-module-which-supports-zipped-eggs

Hey @omab, just wanted to let you know that option 3 worked really well for me. In the spirit of giving back, here's my GitHub OAuth2 class that implements this. It should be useful if anyone asks a question like this in the future:

```python
from social.backends.github import GithubOAuth2 as SocialGithubOAuth2


class GithubOAuth2(SocialGithubOAuth2):
    """
    A GitHub OAuth backend that adds private repo scope if private is in the query string.
    """

    PRIVATE_REPO_SCOPE = 'repo'

    EXTRA_DATA = [
        ('scope', 'scope'),
    ] + SocialGithubOAuth2.EXTRA_DATA

    def get_scope(self):
        scope = super(GithubOAuth2, self).get_scope()
        if self.data.get('private'):
            scope = scope + [self.PRIVATE_REPO_SCOPE]
        return scope

    def extra_data(self, user, uid, response, details=None, *args, **kwargs):
        """
        Checks if the user has enabled private repos and, if so, ensures that their
        private repo access token isn't overridden.
        """
        data = super(GithubOAuth2, self).extra_data(user, uid, response,
                                                    details=details,
                                                    *args, **kwargs)

        social_auth = user.social_auth.filter(provider=self.name).first()
        private_enabled = self._has_enabled_private_repos(social_auth)

        if private_enabled:
            data['access_token'] = social_auth.extra_data.get('access_token')

        return data

    def _has_enabled_private_repos(self, social_auth):
        if social_auth and social_auth.extra_data:
            current_scopes = social_auth.extra_data.get('scope').split(',')
            return self.PRIVATE_REPO_SCOPE in current_scopes
        else:
            return False
```

(I have updated my gist with your Dockerfile as well.) `apt-get install python-pip` will install `pip` for the **system Python**, not the one you've built. What does this mean?

```
$ docker run \
>   --rm \
>   --tty \
>   --interactive \
>   zafields/repro-4352:latest \
>   /bin/bash

Python 2.7.6 (default, Nov  7 2017, 17:21:38)

Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
ImportError: No module named google.protobuf
>>> 
>>> import sys
>>> sys.executable
'/usr/local/bin/python'
>>>
root@694c911e4c13:/projects# /usr/bin/python
Python 2.7.6 (default, Oct 26 2016, 20:30:19)

Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.

>>>
```

----

Also, `apt-get install python-pip` will be managed by the OS, which is why it's in `dist-packages`. You really don't want **any** of your Python packages managed by the OS, you want them managed by you. I.e. even `pip install --upgrade pip` is breaking a contract with `apt` and if bad things happens as a result, it's considered "user error". Notice how in my Dockerfile I've **explicitly** used `python -m pip` instead of `pip`. (One could go even one further and recommend you use `/usr/local/bin/python` rather than `python`.)
